analogy: Think of "Markets Around Us" through the chapter's own opening images: 76 Social and Political Life Markets Around Us We go to the market to buy many things  vegetables, soap, toothpaste, masala, bread, rice, dal, clothes, notebooks, biscuits, etc. If we make a list of the goods that we purchase, it would be really long.... The easy idea is that the visible surface is only the outside cover. The chapter keeps asking how we can know what is inside when no one can directly reach the deep interior. So the learner must read clues: rocks, mines, drilling, volcanic material, heat, gravity, magnetism and earthquake waves. Like solving a hidden-layer puzzle, each clue tells something about the crust, mantle, core and volcanic activity without inventing anything beyond the textbook.
full_notes:
Markets Around Us is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins by asking how the nature of the earth should be imagined and why the interior cannot be known in the same direct way as the surface. It connects the hidden interior with visible results on the surface, especially landscape development, earthquakes, tsunamis and volcanic activity.

The text explains that 76 Social and Political Life Markets Around Us We go to the market to buy many things  vegetables, soap, toothpaste, masala, bread, rice, dal, clothes, notebooks, biscuits, etc.

The text explains that if we make a list of the goods that we purchase, it would be really long.

The text explains that there are many kinds of markets that we may visit for our everyday needs: these can include shops, hawkers stalls in our neighbourhood, a weekly market, a large shopping complex, perhaps even a mall.

The text explains that in this chapter, we look at some of these markets and try to understand how the goods that are sold there reach buyers, who these buyers are, who these sellers are, and the sorts of problems they face.

The text explains that 78 Social and Political Life Why do people go to a weekly market?

The text explains that why dont we find big business persons in these markets?

The text explains that explain with an example how people bargain in the market.

The text explains that can you think of a situation where the bargain would be unfair?

The text explains that sameer is a small trader in the weekly market.

The text explains that he buys clothes from a large trader in the town and sells them in six different markets in a week.

The text explains that he and other cloth sellers move in groups.They hire a mini van for this.

The text explains that his customers are from villages that are near the marketplace.

The text explains that at festival times, such as during Deepavali or Pongal, he does good business.

The text explains that sameer: Seller of clothes Weekly market A weekly market is so called because it is held on a specific day of the week.

The text explains that traders set up shops for the day and then close them up in the evening.

The text explains that then they may set up at a different place the next day.

The text explains that however, we dont buy directly from the factory or from the farm.

The text explains that they use their credit cards to make online purchases. large number of goods are bought and sold that we dont use directly.

The text explains that in what ways is a hawker different from a shop owner?

The text explains that compare and contrast a weekly market and a shopping complex on the following: Market Kind of goods sold Prices of goods Sellers Buyers Weekly market Shopping complex Glossary Weekly market: These markets are not daily markets but are to be found at a particular place on one or maybe two days of the week.

The text explains that these markets most often sell everything that a household needs ranging from vegetables to clothes to utensils.

The text explains that this is usually a large building with many floors that has shops, restaurants and, at times, even a cinema theatre.

The text explains that these shops most often sell branded products.

The text explains that wholesale: This refers to buying and selling in large quantities.

The text explains that most products, including vegetables, fruits and flowers have special wholesale markets.

The text explains that chain of markets: A series of markets that are connected like links in a chain because products pass from one market to another.

The text explains that 4. All persons have equal rights to visit any shop in a marketplace. Do you think this is true of shops with expensive products?

The text explains that 5. Buying and selling can take place without going to a marketplace. Explain this statement with the help of examples.

Source: NCERT Class 7 Social and Political Life II, Chapter 7: Markets Around Us

prelims_traps: Do not treat 76 Social and Political Life and 7 CHAPTER as identical unless the source text links them directly.
Do not add outside examples to Markets Around Us; use only the examples and processes named in the chapter.
Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.
mains_angles: Explain Markets Around Us through the source chain: evidence, process and visible result.
Use 76 Social and Political Life and 7 CHAPTER to show how the chapter connects facts with mechanisms.
Frame an answer around causes, internal working, surface expression and source limitations from the NCERT chapter.

In weekly markets, these shop owners store the things they sell at home.
Most of them are helped by their family members and, hence, do not need to hire workers.
Weekly markets also have a large number of shops selling the same goods which means there is competition among them.
